task_context.description: This is a detector design and performance characterization task for high-energy physics instrumentation. The user wants to design and simulate a homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeter specifically optimized for precision electron energy measurements. The task involves creating a detector geometry using single-material scintillating crystals (no absorber layers), running Monte Carlo simulations with Geant4 to characterize detector performance across multiple electron energies, and comparing different crystal materials to identify the optimal choice for energy resolution. The simulation must measure three key performance metrics: energy linearity, energy resolution, and shower containment efficiency.
task_context.goal: Design an optimal homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeter for precision measurement of electrons by comparing detector materials and geometries to achieve the best energy resolution
constraints: Must use homogeneous design with single dense scintillating crystal as active medium
No absorber material allowed - entire detector volume must be active scintillating material
Cannot use shashlik or accordion geometries (sampling calorimeter designs)
Must use tractable simulation parameters for GPU parallel processing
Must shoot electrons at detector center

data.output:
{'report': "# Homogeneous Electromagnetic Calorimeter Design Study: Material and Geometry Optimization for Precision Electron Energy Measurements

## Abstract

This study presents a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation comparison of homogeneous electromagnetic calorimeters using three scintillating crystal materials (CsI, PbWO₄, BGO) in two geometric configurations (box and projective tower) for precision electron energy measurements. The analysis covers electron energies from 0.5 to 20 GeV
